steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question. Halliwell argues that translating "mimesis" as "imitation" is overly reductive because it implies art merely copies reality, obscuring the multifaceted ways ancient Greeks understood the art-reality relationship. The correct answer must directly support that claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — root word mimos first appeared in drama; mim- root came to be associated with music and poetry: This is about etymology and the word's early usage history, not about what "mimesis" meant philosophically. It says nothing about whether the art-reality relationship was understood in varied ways. ✗
  • Choice B — both Plato and Aristotle addressed mimesis's psychological effects on audiences: This points to one similarity between the two philosophers, not to varied understandings of art's relationship to reality. The issue of psychological effects is separate from whether art merely copies or creates something new. ✗
  • Choice C — Plato discussed aesthetics in early works but didn't use "mimesis" until Cratylus: This is a chronological note about when Plato adopted the term, not about what the term means or how ancient Greeks understood art and reality. ✗
  • Choice D — Plato's writings treat representational art as an inferior reflection of reality, while Aristotle suggests mimesis can refer to art's capacity to envision conditions that could, but don't yet, exist: This directly shows (1) that mimesis means more than simple copying — Aristotle extends it to hypothetical creation — and (2) that ancient Greek philosophers held varied views of the art-reality relationship. Both points support Halliwell's claim. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
